roleFraming: Relationship-First Rep Who Struggles to Close
customerPain: Ethan is a relationship-first sales rep who builds trust and keeps prospects engaged, but struggles when the moment calls for commercial push, urgency, or a direct ask. He struggles with turning positive conversations into decisive next steps. Pipeline looks active, but deals stall before they convert.
fullStory: Ethan is the salesperson prospects like. He brings energy, warmth, and trust into the sales process, which is why conversations feel positive and his pipeline may look healthy. The gap is that the sales motion does not naturally energize him in the same way the relationship does. When the moment requires him to create urgency, ask directly for commitment, hold a commercial line, or move a hesitant prospect to a real next step, he may soften the moment or let it drift. Develop helps his manager coach the specific behavior shift: connect the commercial ask to helping the customer and build the Enterprising Interest, Results Focus, and Influence needed to turn good conversations into closed business.
developAnchorLogic: Use Enterprising Interest as the cleaner primary anchor for the classic likable seller who interviews well, builds relationships, but does not naturally lean into the sales push. Results Focus and Influence can support the coaching story, but Enterprising Interest lets the rep explain the difference between liking people and being energized by commercial action.
selectStory: Ethan is the sales candidate who interviews well because he is likable, relational, and easy to talk to, but he may not naturally lean into the commercial push required to sell.
interviewProbe: Tell me about a time you had to create urgency with a hesitant buyer. What did you say, and what happened next?
selectRisk: He may not be energized by persuasion, competition, urgency, or closing activity, so he can create positive conversations that do not turn into revenue.
developHandoff: If hired, Develop helps the manager coach the shift from relationship-building to commercial action.
useWhenCustomerSays: Our reps interview well but do not close; they are nice but not hungry; pipeline looks good but revenue does not follow.
tdTeamSetup: Sales team or customer-facing revenue team.
tdVisualPattern: Six-person sales team with Ethan + two others lower on Enterprising Interest and three higher on Enterprising Interest.
tdManagerInsight: Different sellers need different motivation. Some reps respond to competition, targets, and earning potential. Others need the commercial action tied to helping the customer or supporting the team.
tdRepTalkTrack: At the individual level, Ethan may not naturally lean into the commercial push. At the team level, this shows how a manager has to coach different motivations toward the same sales outcome: one person may need the scoreboard, while Ethan may need the ask connected to helping the customer move forward.
tdBuildNotes: Keep Ethan relational and likable. Do not make him low across all sales-relevant traits or he will look like a poor sales fit instead of a coachable rep.

customerPain: Derek is a strong live seller who connects well with prospects in the moment, but struggles with the discipline after the call. He does not consistently send follow-ups, log notes, update next steps, or stay on top of open opportunities, so good conversations turn into stalled deals and managers lose visibility into the pipeline.
fullStory: Derek is the rep who can have a great call. He builds rapport, thinks quickly, and prospects often leave the conversation interested. The issue is what happens after the meeting. Follow-up emails go out late or not at all, Salesforce notes are incomplete, and next steps are not always captured while the conversation is fresh. Over time, deals stall, managers cannot trust the forecast, and strong selling moments are wasted because the process around them is inconsistent. Develop helps his manager coach Diligence and Organization and Planning in a practical way: protecting the deals Derek has already created by building repeatable habits for follow-up, documentation, and next-step ownership.
developAnchorLogic: Use Diligence as the primary anchor for sales follow-through and deal hygiene. This is not a closing-confidence problem; it is a process discipline problem after the call. Because Diligence is not on Team Dynamics, use Orderliness or Structured Interest as the team-level proxy for process adherence.
selectStory: Derek is the sales candidate who shines live but may struggle with the process discipline that protects deals after the conversation.
interviewProbe: Walk me through exactly what you do in the 30 minutes after a sales call. How do you document, follow up, and manage next steps?
selectRisk: Follow-up, CRM notes, next steps, and deal hygiene may break down, creating stalled deals and poor forecast visibility.
developHandoff: If hired, Develop helps his manager coach repeatable post-call routines that protect deal momentum.
useWhenCustomerSays: Reps are good on calls but do not follow up; Salesforce is a mess; managers cannot trust the pipeline.
tdTeamSetup: Sales team or account management team focused on deal hygiene and next-step ownership.
tdVisualPattern: Six-person sales team with Derek + two others lower on Orderliness and three higher on Orderliness.
tdManagerInsight: Managers need to know who needs more deal hygiene structure and who can be trusted with more autonomy. Lower-structure reps need post-call routines, CRM expectations, and next-step discipline; higher-structure reps may find too much inspection frustrating.
tdRepTalkTrack: Derek can be strong live with a prospect, but the deals leak after the call. At the team level, Orderliness gives the manager a way to see who needs more structure around follow-up, notes, and next steps, and who may only need outcome-based inspection because they already manage the process well.
tdBuildNotes: Diligence is not a Team Dynamics page. Use Orderliness as the team-level proxy for process adherence; Structured Interest is the alternate for playbook/routine-adherence stories.

roleFraming: Technical Expert Who Creates Team Friction
customerPain: Caleb is a high-skill technical IC whose expertise makes him valuable, but his blunt or impatient style makes collaboration harder than it should be. He struggles with making his knowledge accessible in a way teammates can actually work with. Teammates avoid him, handoffs slow down, and critical knowledge becomes a bottleneck instead of a multiplier.
fullStory: Caleb is one of the sharpest technical people on the team. He solves hard problems, spots issues others miss, and delivers high-quality work, which is why people depend on him. The issue is not capability. It is that his expertise is hard for the rest of the team to access. When teammates do not move at his pace, he can come across as blunt, impatient, or dismissive. Over time, people stop asking questions, handoffs get rough, and the team becomes too dependent on one person. Develop helps his manager coach Compassion in business language: emotional intelligence, communication impact, and how his expertise lands with others, so his technical value becomes easier to share, transfer, and scale.
developAnchorLogic: Use Compassion because it can pull through to Team Dynamics and supports the technical friction story when translated into business language. Avoid moralizing it as whether Caleb cares about people; frame it as emotional intelligence, communication behavior, and whether teammates can access his expertise.
selectStory: Caleb is the technical candidate whose expertise is obvious, but whose communication style may create friction once he has to work cross-functionally.
interviewProbe: Tell me about a time you had to explain a technical issue to someone who did not understand it. How did you handle the gap?
selectRisk: He may be hard to collaborate with, impatient with others, or poor at making his expertise accessible to the team.
developHandoff: If hired, Develop helps the manager coach how his communication lands so his expertise becomes easier for the team to use.
useWhenCustomerSays: Our technical people are smart but hard to work with; knowledge is stuck in one person; handoffs are painful.
tdTeamSetup: Technical team, engineering team, IT team, operations team, or cross-functional project team.
tdVisualPattern: Six-person technical team with Caleb + two others lower on Compassion and three higher on Compassion.
tdManagerInsight: Managers need to coach how communication lands. Lower Compassion can show up as bluntness or impatience; higher Compassion teammates may need more context, care, or emotional awareness in collaboration.
tdRepTalkTrack: Caleb’s expertise is real, but this shows why the team may struggle to access it. He may think he is being efficient or direct, while others experience that as dismissive. The manager can coach communication behavior without diminishing his technical value.
tdBuildNotes: Use business language: communication impact, emotional intelligence, and collaboration behavior. Avoid saying Caleb lacks compassion as a moral judgment.

roleFraming: Skilled Frontline Worker with Inconsistent Execution
customerPain: Riley is a tenured frontline IC with the skill and tribal knowledge the team relies on, but his follow-through is inconsistent. He struggles with the routine discipline required to make his output reliable every day. When he does not carry his part, teammates cover the gaps, tension builds, and managers cannot count on critical work without extra oversight.
fullStory: Riley is a tenured frontline employee with real hands-on skill and tribal knowledge the team relies on. When he is dialed in, he can execute, solve practical problems, and keep work moving. The issue is that the team cannot always count on that version of him. Follow-through, preparation, checklist discipline, or steady productivity can slip, and because he knows the work so well, the inconsistency may have been tolerated longer than it should. When Riley does not carry his part, teammates cover the gaps, tension builds, and managers spend too much time checking work that should already be handled. Develop helps the manager coach the Results Focus gap in a concrete way: expectations, follow-through, productivity, and the habits that make his output reliable again.
developAnchorLogic: Use Results Focus as the cleanest primary anchor for frontline dependability: follow-through, routine discipline, checklist behavior, preparation, attendance-adjacent reliability, and steady productivity.
selectStory: Riley is the frontline candidate who may seem experienced and capable, but carries risk around reliable output, follow-through, and consistency.
interviewProbe: Tell me about a time you had repetitive work or daily production expectations. How did you keep yourself on track when things got busy or boring?
selectRisk: He may not consistently drive tasks to completion, sustain productivity, or carry his part without extra oversight.
developHandoff: If hired, Develop helps the manager coach expectations, follow-through, routines, and reliable output.
useWhenCustomerSays: They know the work but do not always do the work; people can do the job but cannot be counted on; the team covers gaps.
tdTeamSetup: Frontline production, field service, maintenance, warehouse, care team, or technical operations team.
tdVisualPattern: Six-person frontline/technical team with Riley + two others lower on Results Focus and three higher on Results Focus.
tdManagerInsight: Managers need to know who naturally drives output and who needs clearer expectations, milestones, and follow-up. High Results Focus teammates may get frustrated when someone with tribal knowledge is not carrying their part.
tdRepTalkTrack: Riley knows the work, but the team cannot always count on the output. At the team level, Results Focus makes the reliability problem visible: some people naturally push toward completion, while others need clearer expectations and manager follow-up to keep work moving.
tdBuildNotes: Riley's story still uses Diligence language in plain English, but Jess-approved primary is Results Focus. Keep Diligence/Orderliness/Organization and Planning as supporting texture.

customerPain: Isabelle is a caring manager whose team feels supported, but she avoids the direct conversations needed to correct performance. She struggles with turning support into clear expectations and timely accountability. Issues linger, strong employees get frustrated, underperformers do not improve, and accountability feels inconsistent.
fullStory: Isabelle is a deeply supportive manager. Her team feels respected, heard, and cared for. That is real leadership value and part of why people trust her. But when the role requires candid feedback, firmer expectations, or a clear performance conversation, Isabelle waits too long or softens the message. Her intent is to preserve trust, but the result is that underperformance lingers and people miss the feedback they need to grow. Develop helps Isabelle turn care into accountability by giving her one concrete development focus and practical actions for clearer expectations, timely follow-up, and direct but constructive performance conversations.
developAnchorLogic: Use Influence as the primary demo anchor because the pain is not saying what needs to be said to hold team accountable to performance expectations. It connects feedback directly to business outcomes: expectations, performance standards, follow-up, and addressing gaps. Results Focus and Assertiveness support the story but should not lead it.
selectStory: Isabelle is the manager candidate who presents as supportive and people-first, but may struggle to influence others directly when performance gets uncomfortable.
interviewProbe: Tell me about a time someone on your team was underperforming. What did you say, how quickly did you address it, and what changed?
selectRisk: She may avoid direct feedback, soften performance messages, or let underperformance linger.
developHandoff: If hired or promoted, Develop helps the manager turn care into clearer expectations and timely performance coaching.
useWhenCustomerSays: Our managers are too nice; they avoid hard conversations; performance issues linger.
tdTeamSetup: Manager cohort or Isabelle’s direct team.
tdVisualPattern: Six-person manager/leadership team with Isabelle + two others lower on Influence and three higher on Influence.
tdManagerInsight: Managers need to coach directness and accountability without losing trust. Lower Influence managers may avoid hard messages; higher Influence managers may be more willing to say the thing, but may need to tune delivery.
tdRepTalkTrack: Isabelle’s strength is that people feel supported, but the team still needs clear expectations and timely feedback. The Influence view helps explain who naturally speaks up and pushes the conversation forward, and who may need more structure to address performance directly.
tdBuildNotes: Jess update changed the manager accountability story from Results Focus to Influence. Results Focus remains a supporting attribute, but Influence should lead the demo.

roleFraming: Manager Who Becomes the Bottleneck Under Pressure
customerPain: Simon is a capable manager with high standards, but when pressure rises, he tightens his grip instead of steadying the team. He may jump back into doing the work, over-check decisions, or pull approvals back to himself, so execution slows right when the team needs to move faster.
fullStory: Simon is a capable manager with high standards. He knows the work, spots problems quickly, and cares deeply about quality and execution. Those strengths are real. In some cases, this is exactly why someone like Simon was promoted: he was a strong IC who knew how to get things done personally. The gap shows up when pressure rises. When there is a deadline, production issue, customer escalation, staffing gap, or quality concern, Simon can default back to the behavior that made him successful before: taking control, jumping in, checking every detail, or solving the problem himself. When Simon jumps in to control the work — rewriting the deliverable, re-checking every detail, taking the customer call himself — it does not land as support. It lands as a verdict, delivered with visible irritation: you could not handle this. In the short term, it can look like he is protecting quality, but the team learns that direction under stress may not hold and cannot predict which version of Simon they will get, so they default to caution around him. Over time, it slows execution, lowers team ownership, and makes the team dependent on him instead of more capable because of him. Develop helps Simon build Stability in a manager context: staying steady under pressure, resisting the instinct to control every detail, and coaching the team to own more of the work.
developAnchorLogic: Use Stability as the primary anchor because it explains when the bottleneck behavior appears. Simon is not low-quality or careless; pressure makes him reactive and actions are driven by impulse and emotion first, deliberation after the pressure decreases.
selectStory: Simon is the manager or supervisor candidate who looks capable and high-standard, but may become reactive and controlling when pressure rises.
interviewProbe: Tell me about a time your team was under pressure and someone else needed to own an important decision. How did you handle it when the work was not being done the way you wanted?
selectRisk: Under pressure, he may show visible frustration, blame the team, take work back, or create bottlenecks because he does not stay steady enough to coach through the moment.
developHandoff: If hired or promoted, Develop helps him stay steady under pressure and coach the team to own more of the work.
useWhenCustomerSays: Managers become bottlenecks; supervisors jump in instead of leading; teams wait for approval; managers get visibly frustrated under pressure.
tdTeamSetup: Manager cohort, operations leadership team, or frontline leadership team.
tdVisualPattern: Use the Your Team Approach view to show Simon and two others lower on Stability, with Isabelle and two others higher on Stability. Keep extra profiles' Diligence closer to the middle so the visual can be explained through the Stability spread.
tdManagerInsight: Managers need to understand how steadiness changes under pressure. Lower Stability managers may show visible frustration, blame, or reactionary control; higher Stability managers may remain steadier and need to be prompted to notice risks or emotional signals others are reacting to.
tdRepTalkTrack: Simon is capable, but pressure changes how he leads. In the team view, the point is not simply that one person is stressed. It is that managers need different approaches for people who stay steady under pressure versus people whose frustration becomes visible and causes others to become cautious.
tdBuildNotes: Stability is the approved primary attribute, but the Team Dynamics navigation is Getting Work Done Together > Your Team Approach rather than a standalone Stability page. Use the view to tell a low/high Stability story.
